title: Living for God and Suffering as a Christian
subtitle: Arming with Christ's mindset, living for God's will, rejoicing in fiery trials

sections.header: Verses 1-6 - "Living for the Will of God"

heb.paragraph: Arm yourselves with the same thought (ennoia) - Christ's mindset toward suffering becomes the believer's mental armor.
Sensuality, passions, drunkenness, orgies, drinking parties, lawless idolatry - a catalog of pagan vices now abandoned.

heb.gloss: to proclaim good news, preach the gospel
heb.paragraph: The gospel was preached even to the dead - either those who heard while alive but are now dead, or Christ's proclamation to departed spirits.

refs.note: One who has died has been set free from sin.
You must no longer walk as the Gentiles do.
Christ died and lived again, that he might be Lord of both the dead and the living.
We must all appear before the judgment seat of Christ.

notes.note: Arm yourselves with Christ's mindset. Whoever has suffered in flesh has ceased from sin - no longer living for human passions but for God's will.
The time past suffices for doing what Gentiles want to do: licentiousness, passions, drunkenness, orgies. They are surprised you no longer join them.
They will give account to the one ready to judge living and dead. The gospel was preached to the dead so they might live in the spirit.
Calvin interprets ceasing from sin as the mortifying effect of suffering. Affliction weans the soul from earthly attachments.
Calvin understands the dead as those who heard the gospel while alive but have since died. They were judged by men but live before God.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The phrase ceased from sin does not mean sinless perfection but a decisive break with the sinful lifestyle.
The gospel preached to the dead is as difficult as 3:19. Most likely: believers who heard and believed but have since died.

schreiner.source: Thomas Schreiner, NAC 1-2 Peter/Jude — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Schreiner sees the passage as encouragement: pagans will face judgment for their slander. Believers who suffer now will be vindicated.

jobes.source: Karen Jobes, BECNT 1 Peter — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Jobes notes the social pressure: refusal to participate in civic festivals and banquets brought accusations of antisocial behavior.

hist.historical: Peter writes to Christians in five Roman provinces of Asia Minor (modern Turkey) — Pontus, Galatia, Cappadocia, Asia, and Bithynia. These believers face social ostracism and verbal abuse (4:4, 14) rather than official state persecution. As 'elect exiles' (1:1), they experience the marginalization of those who no longer participate in pagan civic religion, trade guild feasts, and family worship. The letter's household codes (2:13–3:7) address how Christians should live within Roman social structures. Peter writes from 'Babylon' (5:13) — almost certainly a symbolic name for Rome. Silvanus (Silas) serves as secretary (5:12), and Mark is present (5:13). The letter likely dates to the early 60s AD, before Nero's persecution.
hist.context: Peter exhorts believers to arm themselves with Christ's mindset toward suffering. Whoever has suffered in the flesh has ceased from sin - suffering produces moral resolve. The time for living in pagan ways is past. Former companions are surprised and malign believers who no longer join them. They will give account to the Judge of living and dead. The gospel was preached to the dead so that though judged in flesh they might live in spirit.

heb.paragraph: Be self-controlled and sober-minded for prayers. Eschatological urgency demands mental discipline.
Show hospitality without grumbling. In a world of traveling Christians, hospitality was essential for mission.
Good stewards of God's varied grace. Gifts are not possessions but trusts to be administered for others' benefit.

refs.note: Having gifts that differ according to the grace given to us.
Varieties of gifts, same Spirit; varieties of service, same Lord.
The coming of the Lord is at hand.

notes.note: The end of all things is at hand. Be sober-minded for prayers. Above all, keep loving one another, for love covers a multitude of sins.
Show hospitality without grumbling. Use gifts to serve one another as good stewards. Speakers speak God's oracles; servers serve by God's strength.
Calvin notes that eschatological expectation should intensify service, not produce paralysis. The imminent end makes present faithfulness urgent.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: Love covers sins - probably quoting Proverbs 10:12. Love does not expose but forgives, maintaining community harmony.
Varied grace (poikilos charis) uses the word for many-colored or variegated. God's grace comes in diverse forms through diverse gifts.

schreiner.source: Thomas Schreiner, NAC 1-2 Peter/Jude — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Schreiner emphasizes that the gifts are oriented to service, not self-display. The goal is God's glory, not personal reputation.

jobes.source: Karen Jobes, BECNT 1 Peter — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Jobes notes that hospitality was countercultural: Christians welcomed strangers regardless of social status.

hist.context: The end of all things is at hand - eschatological urgency shapes ethics. Be sober for prayers; love covers sins; show hospitality; use gifts to serve. Speakers should speak as oracles of God; servers should serve by God's strength. The goal: that God may be glorified through Jesus Christ, to whom belongs glory and dominion forever.

heb.paragraph: The fiery trial (pyrosis) coming upon you - trials are not aberrations but expected tests. Fire refines.
If anyone suffers as a Christian - the term appears only three times in the NT. It was originally a label imposed by outsiders.
Judgment begins with the household of God. If believers face judgment, how much more severe for the disobedient?

refs.note: Count it all joy when you meet various trials.
Blessed are you when others revile you and persecute you on my account.
If the righteous is repaid on earth, how much more the wicked and the sinner!
If children, then heirs - heirs of God and fellow heirs with Christ, provided we suffer with him.

notes.note: Do not be surprised at the fiery trial. Rejoice insofar as you share Christ's sufferings, so that you may rejoice at his revelation.
If insulted for Christ's name, you are blessed. Let none suffer as a murderer or evildoer, but if as a Christian, do not be ashamed - glorify God.
Judgment begins with God's household. If it starts with us, what will be the outcome for those who disobey? If the righteous is scarcely saved, what of the ungodly?
Calvin distinguishes between suffering for wrongdoing (shameful) and suffering for Christ (glorious). The label Christian becomes a badge of honor.
Calvin sees judgment on believers as chastisement, not condemnation. If God disciplines his children, how much more severely will he punish rebels?

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The fiery trial may refer to literal persecution (Nero?) or general suffering. Fire tests and purifies.
Christianos was probably coined in Antioch as a derogatory term. Peter urges wearing it with honor.
Peter quotes Proverbs 11:31 LXX: if the righteous is scarcely saved - not doubting salvation but emphasizing difficulty.

schreiner.source: Thomas Schreiner, NAC 1-2 Peter/Jude — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Schreiner notes that judgment beginning with God's house is purifying, not punitive. Present suffering prepares for eschatological glory.

jobes.source: Karen Jobes, BECNT 1 Peter — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Jobes connects the fiery trial to the refining imagery of 1:7. The metaphor unifies the letter's teaching on suffering.

hist.context: Peter addresses the fiery trial coming upon them. They should not be surprised but rejoice in sharing Christ's sufferings. If insulted for Christ's name, they are blessed - the Spirit of glory rests on them. No one should suffer as a murderer, thief, or evildoer; but suffering as a Christian brings no shame. Judgment begins with God's household; if the righteous are barely saved, what becomes of the ungodly? Let those who suffer according to God's will entrust their souls to a faithful Creator.

themes.note: Judgment is prominent: judgment begins with God's house. Holiness and Justice appear in the call to righteous suffering.

lit.note: Chapter 4 addresses eschatological living: arming with Christ's mindset, using gifts, rejoicing in suffering.

discourse.thesis: Arm yourselves with Christ's mindset — fiery trials are not strange, but share in Christ's sufferings.

nodes.text: Since Christ suffered in the flesh, arm yourselves with the same way of thinking. Live no longer for human passions but for God's will. The Gentiles are surprised you don't join their debauchery — they will give account to the Judge.

The end of all things is at hand. Be self-controlled and sober-minded for prayer. Love covers a multitude of sins. Show hospitality without grumbling. Use your gifts to serve one another — whoever speaks, as one who speaks oracles of God.

Do not be surprised at the fiery trial — as though something strange were happening. Rejoice insofar as you share Christ's sufferings. If you are insulted for the name of Christ, you are blessed. But let none suffer as a murderer or evildoer — if as a Christian, do not be ashamed.

Judgment begins at God's household. If it begins with us, what will be the outcome for those who do not obey? If the righteous is scarcely saved, where will the ungodly and sinner appear? Entrust your souls to a faithful Creator.

coaching.tip: 'Since therefore Christ suffered in the flesh, arm yourselves with the same way of thinking' (v. 1). Suffering requires mental preparation — 'arm yourselves.' Mindset matters: whoever has suffered in the flesh 'has ceased from sin' (v. 1). The body that suffers for Christ isn't simultaneously indulging flesh. Suffering sorts priorities.

'Judgment begins with the household of God' (v. 17). If God's refining fire starts with his own family, what will the end be for those who disobey? Peter uses present suffering as evidence that judgment has begun — not against but for believers. The fire that purifies saints destroys rebels. Same fire, different outcomes.
